statement.markdown: Given a sorted array of integers and an integer target, write a binary search function to find the index of the target. If the target is not present, return -1. You may assume that the array is sorted in ascending order.
statement.inputFormat: Input will be a string with two lines: the size of the array followed by the array elements, and then the target value.
statement.outputFormat: The index of the target if found, and -1 otherwise.
statement.notes: Your function should handle arrays of any size, and the target value may not be present.

examples.explanation: The target 6 is not present in the array

hints.text: Start by considering the smallest possible value for the low pointer.

editorial.title: Binary Search: Incorrect Boundary Check
editorial.intuition: Binary search works by repeatedly dividing the search space in half and searching for the target in one of the two halves.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve iterating through the entire array and checking each element.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution would be to use a binary search algorithm, which has a time complexity of O(log n).
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves initializing the low and high pointers to the start and end of the array respectively, and then repeatedly updating the pointers based on the comparison between the target and the middle element.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the algorithm can be proven using mathematical induction.
editorial.dryRun: To solve a dry run, start by initializing the low and high pointers, and then repeatedly updating the pointers based on the comparison between the target and the middle element.
commonMistakes: Starting with the middle element instead of the smallest possible value for the low pointer.

functionSignature.java: public boolean solve(int[] nums, int target);
functionSignature.python: def solve(self, nums: list[int], target: int) -> bool;
